title: Śakra-stava (Namutthuṇaṁ) — The Seventh Smaraṇa
summary: This is the famous praise of the Arihantas (the awakened, fully-liberated teachers) that, by tradition, Śakra — Indra, the king of the gods — utters when he bows before a Tīrthaṅkara. It is a garland of honour-titles: each line salutes the Arihantas as the path-founders, the best of beings, the givers of fearlessness and of the Dharma, the all-knowing ones who have reached the deathless abode of liberation. A new learner can simply hear it as a heartfelt list of all the reasons the awakened ones are worthy of reverence, framed by two short verses that bow to every Jina-image and to all the liberated souls of past, present, and future.
ritualPlacement: Within this book the Śakra-stava is the seventh of the Nine Smaraṇas (Nava Smaraṇa), the fixed set of nine protective recitations printed inside the Pākṣika (Pakkhī) pratikramaṇa. Because Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī carry no independent text of their own — they are performed exactly as the Pākṣika, only substituting the word 'pakkhī' with 'cāumāsī' or 'saṁvatsarī' and lengthening the kāyotsarga — this same smaraṇa recurs there by reference, not as fresh text. Independently of the smaraṇa-set, Namutthuṇaṁ is also one of the most ubiquitous Śvetāmbara sūtras: it is the Praṇipāta-stava recited at the opening of every caityavandana. As printed here it is bracketed by two standard caityavandana gāthās — 'Jaṁ kiñci nāma titthaṁ' (verse 1), saluting every Jina-image in heaven, the nether worlds, and the middle world, and 'Je a aïā siddhā' (verse 11), saluting all Siddhas of the three times.
authorAndSource: The stava itself is canonical (āgama) rather than the work of a named medieval ācārya; this book lists no individual author for it ('—'). By long tradition the praise is the utterance of Śakra (Indra), and the text is embedded verbatim in the Śvetāmbara Ardhamāgadhī canon — it appears in the Aupapātika (Uvavāiya), Bhagavatī, Jñātādharmakathā and related sūtras as the formula of homage to the Arihantas. The two framing gāthās ('Jaṁ kiñci nāma titthaṁ' and 'Je a aïā siddhā') are standard caityavandana verses of the wider liturgy, not unique to this smaraṇa.
structureArc: Read as eleven displayed verses: verse 1 is the opening caityavandana gāthā 'Jaṁ kiñci nāma titthaṁ', bowing to all Jina-images everywhere. Verses 2–10 are the Namutthuṇaṁ proper — the nine internally-numbered clauses of the Śakra-stava: (v.2 = clause 1) the core salutation 'namotthuṇaṁ arihantāṇaṁ bhagavantāṇaṁ'; (v.3 = clause 2) the Arihantas as path-originators, ford-makers, self-enlightened; (v.4 = clause 3) the 'purisa' (best-of-men) titles — highest of men, lion, white-lotus, fragrant-elephant among men; (v.5 = clause 4) the 'loga' (in-the-world) titles — supreme in, lord of, benefactor of, lamp of, illuminator of the world; (v.6 = clause 5) the five 'givers' — of fearlessness, of sight, of the path, of refuge, of enlightenment; (v.7 = clause 6) the Dharma titles — giver, teacher, leader, charioteer, and four-fold universal-monarch of Dharma; (v.8 = clause 7) possessors of unobstructed supreme knowledge-and-vision who have cast off all veiling (chadma); (v.9 = clause 8) four reflexive-transitive couplets — conquerors and enablers-of-conquest, crossed-over and ferriers, awakened and awakeners, freed and liberators; (v.10 = clause 9) the all-knowing, all-seeing ones who have reached the abode of perfection (siddhi-gati) described by its seven epithets, ending 'namo jiṇāṇaṁ jiabhayāṇaṁ'. Verse 11 is the closing gāthā 'Je a aïā siddhā', a three-fold (tivihena — by mind, speech and body) salute to the Siddhas of past, future, and present.
recurringImagery: The unifying device of this stava is not a single pictorial metaphor but a sustained pattern of grouped honour-titles bound by anaphora (repeated opening words) and by a reflexive-transitive logic. Three clauses cluster by a shared stem — the 'purisa-' titles (v.4), the 'loga-' titles (v.5), and the 'dhamma-' titles (v.7) — each opening with the same word so the listener feels the Arihanta's supremacy first among beings, then in the cosmos, then as sovereign of the Dharma. The 'givers' clause (v.6, '…dayāṇaṁ') and the couplets of v.9 express the stava's deepest theme: the Arihanta has not merely attained a perfection but bestows it — he is himself crossed-over (tinna) and is the ferrier (tāraya) of others, himself awakened (buddha) and the awakener (bohaya), himself freed (mutta) and the liberator (moaga). Two recurring image-words do appear: the Arihanta as lamp/light (loga-païva, loga-pajjoyagara) dispelling the world's darkness, and the four 'best-of-men' animal/flower emblems (lion, white lotus, fragrant elephant). Note that the Saṅgha-as-city / chariot / lotus imagery found elsewhere in this corpus belongs to the Nandī-sūtra sajjhāy and does NOT operate in this sūtra.
keyThemes: Homage to the Arihantas as worthy of supreme reverence
The Tīrthaṅkara as founder of the four-fold path (titthayara)
The Arihanta as giver, not merely possessor, of liberation
Omniscience and the deathless abode of liberation (siddhi-gati)
Conquest of fear and of inner foes (jiṇa, jiabhaya)
Veneration of Jina-images and of all Siddhas across the three times

glossary.definition: 'Praise (stava) of Śakra' — the hymn of homage to the Arihantas traditionally spoken by Śakra/Indra; also called Namutthuṇaṁ or the Praṇipāta-sūtra.
'Reverence be (to…)' — the opening words and common name of this sūtra; from Sanskrit namo'stu.
Arhat — the 'foe-destroyer', one who has annihilated the inner enemies (passions) and attained omniscience; the awakened, living, fully-enlightened teacher.
'Ford-maker' — an Arihanta who founds the four-fold congregation (saṅgha) and re-establishes the path across the ocean of rebirth.
A protective recitation 'to be remembered'; this stava is the seventh of the nine Smaraṇas recited in the Pākṣika pratikramaṇa.
'Self-awakened' — one who attains enlightenment by himself, without instruction from another (Prakrit sayaṁsaṁbuddha).
'Highest among men' — first of the four 'purisa' (best-of-men) epithets in verse 4, alongside lion, white-lotus, and fragrant-elephant of men.
'Supreme in the world' — opening epithet of the 'loga' cluster (v.5): supreme in, lord of, benefactor of, lamp of, and illuminator of the world.
Enlightenment / right insight (Prakrit bohi); the Arihanta is bohi-daya, the 'giver of enlightenment' (v.6).
The teaching and right order proclaimed by the Jina; in v.7 the Arihanta is its giver, teacher, leader, charioteer (sārathi), and four-quartered universal monarch (cakravartin).
'Universal monarch of the four quarters' — here a metaphor: the Arihanta is the sovereign wheel-turner of the Dharma, dharma-vara-cāuraṁta-cakkavaṭṭi.
The state of being veiled by destructive karmas; 'viaṭṭa-chaüma' (v.8) means one who has cast off this veil — possessing unobstructed supreme knowledge and vision (kevala-jñāna and kevala-darśana).

The 'destination of perfection' — the abode of the liberated, qualified in v.10 by seven epithets: śiva (blissful), acala (immovable), aruja (free of disease), ananta (endless), akṣaya (imperishable), avyābādha (untroubled), apunarāvṛtti (no return to rebirth).
'By the three-fold means' — i.e. with mind, speech, and body; the manner of the closing salute to all Siddhas (v.11).

howToRead: A newcomer can read this simply as a litany of honour-titles — let each phrase name one more reason the awakened ones are revered, and notice the framing verses that bow to all Jina-images (v.1) and all liberated souls (v.11). An advanced practitioner should attend to the grouped anaphora (purisa-, loga-, dhamma- clusters) and especially the reflexive-transitive couplets of v.9, where the Arihanta is both the one who has attained and the one who carries others across.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim sūtra text, placement of the Śakra-stava as the seventh of the Nine Smaraṇas within the Pākṣika pratikramaṇa, and orthography.

sources.title: The Aupapâtika Sûtra (Uvavāiya), ed./tr. tradition of the Śvetāmbara Ardhamāgadhī canon
sources.consultedFor: Canonical attestation of Namutthuṇaṁ as the formula of homage to the Arihantas embedded in the āgamas.

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Hindi dictionary), H. D. T. Sheth
sources.consultedFor: Glosses of Prakrit terms (sayaṁsaṁbuddha, viaṭṭa-chaüma, tivihena, the siddhi-gati epithets).

notes: Verse numbering follows the book's printed convention, which displays eleven units: the bracketing caityavandana gāthās 'Jaṁ kiñci nāma titthaṁ' (shown as '(1)') and 'Je a aïā siddhā' (shown as '(1)') enclose the nine internally-numbered clauses (1)–(9) of the Namutthuṇaṁ proper; mapped to a flat list these are verses 1, 2–10, and 11 respectively. The Śakra-stava carries no named individual author in this book; the attribution to Śakra/Indra is traditional, and its canonical home is the Śvetāmbara āgama corpus — stated here without claiming a single redactor. Spellings are preserved verbatim from the book (e.g. 'અતિયાર'-style conventions elsewhere in the corpus; here note 'સયંસંબુદ્ધાણં', 'પજ્જો અગરાણં', 'મવ્વાબાહ મપુણરાવિત્તિ' as printed, including word-internal spacing). The seven epithets of the liberated abode (śiva … apunarāvṛtti) are sometimes counted slightly differently in other editions; this preface follows the words as printed. Source URLs are given at the level of a verified repository homepage rather than risking an unverified deep link; treat exact page anchors as the book's own (book pp. 54–103, Pakkhī section).
